sentences.text: Ernst Hacker-- E-R-N-S-T H-A-C-K-E-R.
I was born April 29, 1929, in Schwarzenbach, S-C-H-W-A-R-Z-E-N-B-A-C-H, Schwarzenbach, Wienerneustatt, W-I-E-N-E-R-N-E-U-S-T-A-T-T.
Austria.
Vienna, Austria.
My parents from my father's side.
My father was born in Schwarzenbach, too, in Austria.
His family, his father and grand family, 400 years back, were born all in the same neighborhood.
There's gravestones in [NON-ENGLISH]-- that is next to Schwarzenbach-- that have the Hackers, for 400 years, stones.
That the people-- my family-- was born there.
My mother's side came from Luckenbach.
That is in [NON-ENGLISH], closer to the Hungarian border.
I don't know how long they were there, but I know my grandpa from my mother's side was born there, too.
I knew my grandmother very good.
My grandfather from the-- from my parents-- the-- from my mother, the grandmother I knew very good.
They lived in Luckenbach.
There was a big Jewish community.
They had a big temple, synagogue.
And I remember, as a child, when we went there, the women was separate, and the men were separate.
And from my grandmother in Luckenbach, I always remember, when they came there, she made noodle soup-- chicken noodle soup.
And she made the noodles, everything, by herself.
She cut the noodles very thin.
And I never forget the taste of it.
But Schwarzenbach was a little village.
A farmer village, actually.
And we were only the two Jewish families in the whole village.
That-- the name was Holzer, the other Jewish family, and the Hackers.
That were the only two Jewish family in the village of Schwarzenbach.
Schwarzenbach is a little village with farmers, surrounded by mountain and forests.
In the back of our house, we had a little lake.
Not like a lake-- running water, like a--
No, it's water running.
River.
A little river.
And right behind our house was a little bridge, and the water was standing there and running over the--
Like a dam.
And as children, I remember we always used to, for my mother's washing thing.
She had a wooden-- a wooden box, where she was washing with the kids--
Yes.
But we used it to go on this lake and to play there.
We had a big house.
Actually, this house was built by my father and by his brother.
My grandparents from father's side, they had a little, old shack.
And when my father later on came, and his brother, Max, they built a three-story house.
The down-- in the down floor was a bakery, and the front of the house was a store that we sold bakery goods, food, material for clothing, everything.
It's a general store.
And in the house we had two parts.
In one side was my uncle living.
And on one side, my parents lived when I was born.
We had a special kitchen for Passover on our side, in the house.
And then, later on, I-- three years, later my brother was born, Leo.
When he was born, I was already three years old.
I remember he was born in Vienna-- I was born in Schwarzenbach.
Not by a doctor, by a hebamme they called it in German.
A woman.
A midwife-- brought me to world.
And I was standing and waiting for him and my mother to come back.
It was a beautiful place, with-- everything was green.
Forests, flowers, snow in the winter.
But in-- in Schwarzenbach, there was no synagogue because of two Jewish families.
And-- but we had kosher in our house.
All Jewish families were very religious-- not fanatic, but we were religious.
At Passover, we had kosher food that was brought in from Vienna.
And when I started later on to go to school, when there was a religious class for the other children, I-- I went out.
And I went to [NON-ENGLISH] then I became, already, six years old, seven years old, to the next village where there was a big synagogue.
And I took religious classes there.
Walking distance, about 20 minutes. 20, 25 minutes.
Was a big village there.
Meet a lot of Jewish families, with restaurants, with everything.
On Jewish holidays, we always went to my grandma, to Luckenbach.
Was a big Jewish community there.
Even in winter, I remember when the cars couldn't go, we always went with horse.
We had a special wagon for winter.
Sled with the horses.
And we went to our grandmother there.
We had a family-- my mother's side, she had a lot of brothers and sisters.
One of the brothers was a shoemaker that had, later on, not when I was born, but he had a family of 10 to 12 children.
The other one, this was Auntie Uncle Iula-- Uncle Morritz.
The other brother was a shoemaker, that he had eight or 10 children.
And she had a sister, Emma, that was married without children.
From father side, there was Uncle Willie and Auntie Paula-- they had three sons almost at my age.
One-- one son was Walter, Erich, and Kurt.
And Kurt-- he was the same age that my younger brother.
Walter was my age.
Then we had a-- another uncle, Uncle Shanda, with his wife.
I don't remember if he had children.
He had a sister, Fluss, Malvin Fluss, that she had three children, Arthur, Elsa, and Erich, too.
Then he had another brother, Uncle Philip, with Aunt Rosa.
That they had-- he had two sons and a daughter.
For Passover, from Schwarzenbach we went only our family.
The other family, all the other brothers from my father's side, they lived in Vienna.
Except one sister, Auntie Emma, she was in Schwarzenbach too, living during the time I was living there.
Yeah, I enjoyed it very much.
I remember the Passovers.
The only thing, I never had patience to wait, to-- to get to the meal.
It took a long time.
Till all the prayers was done.
My father and my uncle.
I had an Uncle Max was his name, and Aunt Regine-- they lived together like one family.
He had one son.
And I-- I remember, the only thing we had, the kids, we had a bike that my cousin used, and I used.
We didn't have too many toys.
We were not rich, but we were rich in another way.
We had-- we were a very happy family.
The anti-Semitism, I can't remember.
Before Hitler came, I can't remember no anti-Semitism
We were all like a family.
The neighbors, my father, we were all like a big family.
The whole village.
My father deal with-- with fruit, too.
He bought from the farmers apples, fruits.
He picked it from the-- from the trees.
We had horses in our home, we had chickens.
We had everything.
We had a three-- third-story house that we had was in summer, sick children from Vienna came to visit us.
Even we were not rich, but there was always room for 10, 20 children that came for vacation.
We speak Austrian.
German Austrian.
At home.
But it's a little bit different than the Germans.
Because later on, when I met Germans, it was very hard for me to understand them.
It's a dialect.
You know, farmers dialect, or Viennese.
But this was a little bit, even, different, because it's a dialect that, in little villages and Austrians that-- actually, when you write it, it's German.
But when you speak it, it's a little bit different.
I didn't speak Yiddish, no.
The only thing I knew is Viennese Austrian.
And we started, when I went once or twice in a week, when I went to the other village for religious lessons, we were starting to read the Bible in Hebrew.
But I could read it, but I didn't understand what I was reading.
I enjoyed learning Hebrew.
I enjoyed to learn about-- a little bit about history.
And I enjoyed, at home, the religious holiday.
Especially when we went to the temple, to my grandma, to Luckenbach.
When we were together with the whole family, and I get my noodle soup, and my special food.
It was very-- a very happy time.
And all my childhood, in Schwarzenbach was a very healthy childhood.
From school.
I had a lot of non-Jewish friends-- neighbors, and-- and from school, girls and boys.
And we had a good time, always.
In winter or summer, we're-- we were going in the mountains, we were sliding down the snow hills.
And in summer, too, many times I went with my father, with the horses to bring fruit, or wood he need for the bakery.
Or he dealt with-- with the lumber, too.
So we had to go to other villages, like Luckenbach, Deutschkreutz, to different places.
So he loaded the wagon with the-- with lumber very high.
And I, with him, was sitting on top and going to the other villages.
We had really-- it was very funny, very fun for me.
And then when my brother came, Leo, I was not alone.
And I was so happy to have a little brother.
His name was Leo.
He was born January 16, 1932.
He was a very quiet guy, later on-- later on in the years.
But in the beginning, when he was a baby, we had a lot of fun.
I was very happy to have a little brother.
And then the Holzer family-- they had a little girl.
Her name was Litza, and her brother was Kurt Holzer.
That we were many times, they came to our house, and we visited them.
Then we had dogs.
We had animals.
They had dogs.
We had dogs.
And we wandered around to the forest with the dogs.
And we had, really, a good-- good time all together.
My-- in our house, we had a schedule.
My father actually was the baker.
His brother was more the businessman.
Then later on, he bought a truck to bring the fruit and everything to Wienerneustatt for sale.
My aunt Regine, that she in-- she was more in the store.
And my mother was the house maker.
She was known, anyway, in the whole family, as the cook-- the special cook.
But she-- she arranged-- was arranging the house.
We had some people in the house working that helped my father in the bakery, and they helped in the store, and they helped to go to the forest to bring wood.
So my mother was actually arranging everything in the house, and to cook.
And next to the kitchen, we had a special room for food that was stored everything in the cellar.
We had a special machine that made fruit drinks from the apples.
That we made our own-- Like cider?
Yes.
And all this I helped my father.
I was running with him.
But one of the things I never forget, when we were little-- my father was crazy after the children.
Later on, I found out that when I was born in 1929-- actually, my father, that he was born in 1982-- in 1882, he was already--
He was already in his 40s-- oh, 47.
And my little brother was born, he was 50, around 50.
So for him, whatever we asked, we were like his gods.
He loved us so much.
He didn't know what to do with us.
But every morning, when we came to eat breakfast, he always made us little animals from the bread.
He made us a little dog, a little mouse, a little-- always we found on our table-- to eat, you know, like salmon.
You know, like the rolls.
He made us all kinds of things in the bakery.
And there was no-- not a morning that you didn't have nothing special done for us.
And he was very friendly with our neighbors.
They all knew us.
They all know Samuel.
They called him-- my father's name was Samuel.
And they called him Samuel-- Samuel.
And he was friends with everybody.
All the time-- there was the neighbors came to our house, and we came to the neighbors.
It was a very friendly relations between the whole village.
My father was in World War I, in the Austrian army, for many years.
And as he told me later, it was a very hard time.
During the war, World War I, it was very hard.
There was times they didn't have food.
They told me when horses died, they had to cut them in pieces and eat it-- eat the meat.
It was a very hard war.
And it was very hard for him in the army.
And my uncle, too.
Everybody was in the army in World War I.
If he was more Austrian than Jewish?
He felt that he's an Austrian.
I mean, he fought for the Austrian army, and he was born there.
All his grand-- grand-- grandparents were born there.
And we lived like Jewish people.
I mean, it was kosher in the house.
But he felt that he's an Austrian because his background-- he was born there, his grandfather was born there.
He went to school there.
But it was a religious home, because it was-- it had to be.
I mean, if you were Jewish that time, you had to be religious.
If somebody wouldn't be kosher, that was not normal, because wherever we went in the other village, in Luckenbach, everybody was kosher.
Over there, they were more religious than in our house because we were only two Jewish families in Schwarzenbach.
In Luckenbach, or [NON-ENGLISH], they had synagogues, they had their rabbis.
We had to go there.
But it was-- we were religious at home, and-- but he felt that he was born there, and he's Austrian.
When Hitler came to power, it started, I think, in 1939.
Not in Austria.
When Hitler came to power in Germany, but we didn't feel it.
I didn't know about it.
And-- and I don't think that my parents were afraid of it, or had a feeling that something would happen to them.
And I went into school, and still, till Hitler came, even if I went to school on religious hours, I left and went to the other village afternoon, and I went to religious school there.
But when Hitler came, I think it was 1939.
Because when I got my school report, in the moment Hitler came, they put next to my name, Hacker, Ernst, Israel.
In the moment Hitler came, we became already to be Jewish, known.
And we had everybody in the village that knew us, already knew that we were Jews.
And, I mean, they knew before.
But even in the school, that the only one that went to school there was my brother, me, and Litza.
Her brother was, I think, too young.
And right away, in '39, they put in our report card, Israel.
And to the girls, Sarah.
As far as I remember, first of all, when Hitler came, right away, they arrested, first, my father.
My father was arrested, and after two, three days, he came back.
And they said they arrested me because I'm Jewish.
But they told me, you are not a Jew.
You don't look like a Jew.
You are not a Jew.
Go home.
And he came home again.
Me, my brother, and the other kids, in the moment Hitler came, they already called us dirty Jews.
It was something like we were in a different world.
My best friends, my neighbors, the children started calling me [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Dirty Jew.
They called us right away pig Jew, dirty Jew.
And suddenly, nobody was our friend anymore.
Everybody that was our friends before, suddenly, everything had changed.
It made me feel terrible.
I-- I cried.
We were only home.
We were afraid to go out of the house.
And I-- I didn't go to school anymore.
I was afraid to go to school, because the kids start hitting us.
They start calling us names.
And nobody was anymore any friend.
Nobody that we all were friends before, suddenly, nobody knew us anymore.
But weeks later, they came.
Yeah-- what I didn't tell you, when they-- when my father and my uncle built their house, they probably borrowed some money to build it.
And when Hitler came, suddenly they came and signed my father and my uncle that they have to sell the house.
They owed them maybe $500 on it, to different people for lumber, or I don't know-- for different-- very small amounts, that I found out after the war.
It was dollars.
They made him sign.
They forced him to sign that he has to sell our house.
And we had to leave our house.
The Austrian, the German police.
The police from the village.
If it was the police, I-- I don't know.
If they came and told him, you have to leave the house, you sign, you owe money, you have to leave your house.
So we had to pack everything.
And we couldn't take furniture, we couldn't take-- we took out whatever we had.
We had some suitcases.
And my uncle, my father, my uncle's wife, and the son, Fritz, and all on a truck.
And we went to Vienna.
What I never will forget, we had a dog in our home called Digger, a-- a shepherd-- German shepherd.
His color was beige.
And when we left the village with the truck, the dog ran after us maybe 10, 20 miles.
And later on, they-- we got a-- we heard that the dog died in the village.
He didn't eat, and he died because we left.
Coming on to Vienna, I remember, we rented a three-bedroom apartment in Sweitenbitzirgen, the [NON-ENGLISH].
I will never forget it.
The apartment consisted of a living room, and three bedrooms, and a kitchen.
When we came to Vienna, we had already to wear a yellow star, written on it, Jude.
We had to go to the Jewish community there.
It was, I think, in 1940.
Beginning '40, we had to leave our home and go to Vienna.
We had to register in the Jewish community there.
The [GERMAN] they called it in Vienna.
We got the-- the star of David that was written on it, Jude-- Jew.
And we looked for the apartment, and we have found the apartment.
But we had to move in there.
Our family, my mother, my father, brother, and me, in the other room, one room, moved in Malvin Fluss-- my brother's sister, with their three children.
My father's sister.
In the other room was another sister-- No-- another--from my father's brother, the wife, Rosa Hacker.
And a cousin and his sister moved in, too.
All of us, we moved in, in one apartment in Vienna.
And right on the beginning, my father on the beginning start looking for work, and found a job as a baker in a-- In a bakery.
And life was not easy.
We-- we kids, we had to go only to a Jewish school.
They had it in the 20-- in [GERMAN] in the 20 [GERMAN].
It was a different--
Section of town.
We were in the second section.
This was in the 20th section, that were only for Jewish children.
My father worked.
My mother was home.
It was not easy, because food you couldn't get.
You got special coupons for food.
And we didn't get the same coupons than the Aryan-- they called it, the Gentiles.
For the Jewish people, was special rationing.
But because my father worked in the bakery, so bread was no problem.
We had bread to eat.
Less rationing than the Gentiles, yes.
We had a lot of problems to get food.
But bread, we had.
So this was the most important thing.
In the school, it was not bad.
We had good teachers.
We had good starting to learn.
I start to belonging to a group of Jewish children.
It was a Zionist organization, they called it in Vienna, [GERMAN].
And I went there twice a week.
We had-- we got a lot of education of, then, they called it Palestine.
We learned songs about Palestine.
And we had--
I was then-- this was in 1940, '41.
I was around 12 years old.
The time went by.
We had a lot of relatives in Vienna.
My uncles from my mother's side, with all the children, they had to leave Luckenbach.
The little village where the synagogue was.
They all came to Vienna.
They all, with the children, and had little apartments.
The tailor was working as a tailor.
The shoemaker was working as a shoemaker.
Then we had another uncle that had the three children, Walter, Erich, and Kurt, that had a restaurant.
And as well known my mother was as a cook, everybody came always to-- to us, in our apartment.
They brought some food, and my mother cooked.
And everybody, especially the children-- they always came-- Tante Adele.
They called my mother Tante Adele.
